sentence: Subjective:
The patient, Jordan Roberts, is a 49-year-old male with a history of hypertension and palpitations.
He presents with resistant hypertension, despite being on three medications at maximum dose.
He reports having a few cups of coffee and no breakfast on the day of his previous emergency room visit, which he attributes to a panic attack.
His girlfriend and he discussed it, and he believes it was not related to his blood pressure.
He mentions a family history of high blood pressure and reports that his new primary doctor started him on a new medication, Benicar HCT (hydrochlorothiazide), one month ago.
He is also taking Amlodipine 10 mg daily.
He denies any issues with his salt intake, stating he is doing well with it.
He reports trying to walk on a treadmill when he can.
He has a potassium level of 4.0 from one week ago, but it has not been rechecked recently.
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure has been running at 146-155 lately, not yet in the 120s.
His potassium level is 4.0.
Physical exam: His heart and lungs sound good.
There is no tenderness or swelling in his ankles or feet.
Test Results: Not mentioned.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has resistant hypertension, despite being on three medications at maximum dose, and is a candidate for further evaluation and treatment.
Problem list: Hypertension; Suspected resistant hypertension; Hypokalemia; Hyperaldosteronism; Potential for electrolyte imbalance; Potential for dehydration; Potential for gynecomastia.
Plan:
I added Spironolactone to his regimen to help lower his blood pressure.
I will monitor his potassium and sodium levels in 2 weeks.
He will have a basic metabolic panel and blood pressure check in 2 weeks.
If he is not controlled on Spironolactone, I will refer him to the resistant hypertension clinic for further evaluation and potential treatment, including renal denervation.
Follow-up:
I will see him in 2 weeks to check his blood pressure and electrolytes.
